blocks.title: Overview
blocks.text:
The workshop now pivots outward — from the leader's interior life to the young people and communities they serve. This pivot is not a departure from the self-development work of Sessions 1–8; it is its destination. DVULI's conviction is that healthy leaders build healthy organizations that serve healthy communities, and that the chain cannot be skipped. Having examined who they are and what Core Values they will live by, participants are now equipped to look at their youth and their communities through a transformed lens. Session 9 provides the first lens: the 40 Developmental Assets framework developed by the Search Institute of Minneapolis.

The 40 Developmental Assets represent five decades of research into what building blocks are necessary for young people to grow up healthy, make wise choices, and become contributing community members. The assets are divided into External Assets (Support, Empowerment, Boundaries and Expectations, Constructive Use of Time — the things that communities, families, and organizations provide to youth) and Internal Assets (Commitment to Learning, Positive Values, Social Competencies, Positive Identity — the strengths that young people develop from within). The research finding that undergirds the entire framework is simple and sobering: the more assets a young person has, the less likely they are to engage in risky behaviors and the more likely they are to thrive. Most young people in under-resourced urban communities have far fewer than the 31 assets that represent a threshold for positive development.

Participants in this session rate three representative youth from their ministries — one who is thriving, one who is participating inconsistently, and one who displays multiple risk behaviors — across all 40 assets. This rating exercise produces a profound insight: the difference between the thriving youth and the struggling youth is rarely a moral failure on the part of the struggling youth but a deficit of the relational, organizational, and community resources that produce assets. This reframe — from 'youth as problem' to 'youth as problem-solvers lacking resources' — is the central conceptual shift that Community Youth Development demands. It connects directly to the Consequential Faith framework introduced by Kenda Creasy Dean in Almost Christian: thriving adolescents in faith have a creed to believe, a community to belong to, a call to live out, and a hope to hold onto.
blocks.html:
Session 9

What Does It Take to Grow Up Healthy?

Turning the lens outward — using the 40 Developmental Assets to reframe how we see young people and the communities they deserve

When Danny Agosto was sixteen, local police considered him one of the most dangerous teenagers in Jackson. A gang runner, a dealer, a dropout — at least that is what every label attached to him said. What those labels never captured was the deficit on the other side of the ledger: an absent father, a mother working third shift at a packaging plant forty-five minutes outside the city, a neighborhood without a single safe adult who stayed around long enough to matter. It was not a moral failure that put Danny on those streets. It was a catastrophic shortage of the developmental resources that every young person requires.

Years later, standing at a podium accepting the Jackson Hispanic Chamber of Commerce Legacy Leadership Award, Danny Agosto told the room: "I didn't care about anyone else's life because I didn't value my own. I was just searching for the love and acceptance I never got at home." His mentor Juan Mendoza had not lectured Danny into transformation. He had noticed a gift, offered an opportunity, and supplied what the community had withheld. He had, in the language of the research you are about to engage, built assets. This session will give you a scientific language for what Mendoza did — and a structured framework for doing it intentionally with every young person in your ministry.

The Pivot: From the Inside Out

Sessions 1 through 8 of DVULI have asked you to look inward — at your calling, your character, your behavioral style, your leadership strategy, the Core Values you are committing to live by, and the personal patterns that produce breakthrough or breakdown. That interior work was not preliminary to the real work. It was foundational to it. DVULI's conviction is clear: healthy leaders build healthy organizations that serve healthy communities, and no stage of that chain can be skipped.

Session 9 marks the pivot outward. Having examined who you are at your best, you are now equipped to look at your youth and your communities through a transformed lens. The first lens DVULI places in your hands is one of the most rigorously validated frameworks in the field of adolescent development: the 40 Developmental Assets, developed by the Search Institute of Minneapolis over five decades of research involving millions of young people across North America and beyond.

Think About It

Before reading further, bring three specific young people from your ministry to mind — one who is clearly thriving, one who participates inconsistently, and one who displays multiple risk behaviors. Hold their faces in your mind as you work through this material. The research will explain far more about the differences between them than you might expect.

Five Decades of Research on What Works

The Search Institute launched its asset-based research in 1989, initially surveying 47,000 sixth through twelfth graders in 111 communities across the United States. What they discovered was both simple and paradigm-shifting: the more developmental assets a young person has, the less likely they are to engage in risk behaviors — alcohol and drug use, violence, early sexual activity, school dropout — and the more likely they are to demonstrate what the researchers called thriving indicators, including academic achievement, leadership, and helping others (Benson, Scales, Leffert, & Roehlkepartain, 1999).

This finding, replicated across surveys of more than three million young people in thousands of communities, established what researchers call a dose-response relationship: more assets produce better outcomes, and the relationship is remarkably consistent regardless of gender, geography, race, or socioeconomic background (Scales & Leffert, 2004). Crucially, this is not merely a proprietary Search Institute construct. Scales and Leffert (2004) conducted a comprehensive synthesis of hundreds of peer-reviewed studies and found that each of the 40 assets is independently validated by the broader scientific literature on adolescent development. The assets reflect scientific consensus, not branded methodology.

The sobering underside of this finding is equally consistent: most American young people — and particularly young people in under-resourced urban communities — report having far fewer than half of the 40 assets. The Search Institute identified 31 assets as a threshold associated with robust positive development. In national surveys, the average young person reports having only 18 to 22 (Scales & Leffert, 2004). In many of the communities where DVULI leaders serve, that number is lower still. The gap between what young people have and what they need is not a character gap. It is a community resource gap — and that reframe is the central conceptual shift of this session.

The Architecture of the Framework: Eight Categories, Two Domains

The 40 assets are organized into two primary domains — External Assets and Internal Assets — and eight subcategories of four each. Understanding this structure is essential for ministry application because it maps directly onto the dual responsibilities of the community and the individual young person.

External Assets: What Communities Provide

External assets are the relational and structural building blocks that families, organizations, congregations, schools, and neighborhoods provide to young people. They represent the environmental conditions that either support or undermine healthy development. There are four external asset categories:

  • Support (Assets 1–6): The experience of being loved, valued, and cared for by family, teachers, other adults, and neighbors. This includes the simple but powerful presence of adults who know a young person's name and communicate positive regard.
  • Empowerment (Assets 7–10): The experience of being seen as a resource rather than a problem — being asked to contribute, serve, and lead. Safety is also an empowerment asset; young people cannot be empowered in environments where physical threat is constant.
  • Boundaries and Expectations (Assets 11–16): Consistent, caring, and clearly communicated boundaries at home, school, and in the neighborhood, combined with high expectations from adults who model positive behavior.
  • Constructive Use of Time (Assets 17–20): Three or more hours per week of structured, creative, or enriching activities through religious communities, youth programs, and arts or sports — activities that crowd out the dangerous void of unstructured time.

Internal Assets: What Young People Develop

Internal assets are the values, skills, and self-perceptions that young people build through experience, relationship, and intentional formation. They do not emerge in a vacuum — they are cultivated by the same communities that provide external assets. There are four internal asset categories:

  • Commitment to Learning (Assets 21–25): Motivation to achieve, engagement at school, completion of homework, bonding to educational institutions, and the practice of reading for pleasure.
  • Positive Values (Assets 26–31): The internalization of caring, social justice, integrity, honesty, responsibility, and restraint as lived commitments rather than merely stated ideals.
  • Social Competencies (Assets 32–36): Practical skills for navigating relationships and environments — planning, interpersonal communication, cultural competence, the ability to resist negative peer pressure, and peaceful conflict resolution.
  • Positive Identity (Assets 37–40): The bedrock of healthy development: a sense of personal power, healthy self-esteem, a clear sense of purpose, and a positive view of one's own future.

Workshop Exercise: The 40 Assets Youth Profile Rater

The following interactive tool is central to Session 9. Following the methodology described in your DVULI Pre-Assignment for Local Workshop 2, you will now rate three young people from your ministry across all 40 assets. Use the three-point scale: 3 = clearly present, 2 = somewhat present, 1 = clearly lacking. Enter brief labels for each youth (you may use initials or descriptors like "Youth A – Thriving"). As you rate each asset, the bar chart at the bottom builds dynamically, revealing the distribution of assets across your three youth profiles and generating a gap analysis.

40 Assets Youth Profile Rater

Y1  Y2  Y3  — Rate each asset: 3=Clearly Present, 2=Somewhat Present, 1=Clearly Lacking

Reading the Results: What the Ratings Reveal

If you have completed the rating exercise honestly, something important likely just happened. Look at the gap between your thriving youth and your high-risk youth. Now ask yourself: is the difference primarily explained by the character of those two young people? Or is it primarily explained by the community resources available to each of them?

Peter Benson, the founder of the Search Institute and architect of the assets framework, argued compellingly in All Kids Are Our Kids (2006) that this is not a question about individual moral failure — it is a question about community investment. Thriving youth do not simply decide to thrive. They are embedded in relational and organizational environments that actively build the building blocks of healthy development. Struggling youth are not deficient in character; they are deficient in assets. This is not a diminishment of personal responsibility; it is a clarification of where responsibility lies in the first place.

"Asset building is everybody's work. It is the work of families, of congregations, of neighborhoods, of schools, of youth organizations, of employers — of every sector of community life. No single institution can build all 40 assets for any young person."
— Benson (2006, p. 67)

The research of Scales, Roehlkepartain, and Shramko (2017) adds a particularly important nuance for faith-community leaders: caring adults outside the family — including church leaders, coaches, and mentors — are among the most powerful asset-building forces available to young people, and their impact is disproportionately significant for youth in under-resourced communities where family assets may be limited. Danny Agosto did not need his mentor Juan Mendoza to be everything. He needed Mendoza to be one adult who noticed his gift and stayed around long enough to use it.

Think About It

Looking at your gap analysis from the rater above: which single asset category shows the widest gap between your thriving youth and your high-risk youth? What specific actions — already within your ministry's capacity — could begin to close that gap within the next 30 days?

The Balance That Cannot Be Ignored: External vs. Internal Assets

One of the most practically important insights of the assets framework is the equal weight given to both external and internal domains. Faith communities sometimes err in one of two directions, and both errors produce predictable failures:

The first error is to focus exclusively on external asset provision — programs, services, safe spaces, food, tutoring — without investing in the development of internal assets. The result, as Benson (2006) documents, is young people who are dependent on external support rather than equipped to navigate life without it. This is the betterment trap that the Community Youth Development framework described in Local Workshop 2 cautions against: doing things for youth rather than with them.

The second error is to focus exclusively on internal asset development — character formation, faith instruction, values training — while disregarding the environmental conditions that either support or undermine those internal capacities. A teenager who is taught the value of integrity in a Sunday school classroom, then released into a neighborhood with no safety, no adult role models, no structured activities, and no sense that the community values them, is being set up to fail against environmental odds. Internal assets do not fully develop in external deserts.

The research base is unequivocal on this point: both domains matter, and they are mutually reinforcing (Catalano, Berglund, Ryan, Lonczak, & Hawkins, 2004). The most effective positive youth development programs identified in Catalano et al.'s systematic review of 25 programs were those that addressed both domains simultaneously, integrating relational and organizational provision with the intentional cultivation of values, skills, and identity in young people. The framework calls us to both/and thinking, not either/or programming.

Think About It

Reflect honestly on your current ministry programming. Does it lean more heavily toward external asset provision or internal asset development? What would need to change to achieve a more intentional balance? Complete the widget below before forming your answer.

External vs. Internal Assets Balance Visualizer

Each card below is one of the 40 Developmental Assets. Click a card to toggle it between External (things communities provide) and Internal (strengths young people develop). Sort all 40, then click Reveal Results to see the correct 20/20 split and which ones you may have misclassified — and why the balance matters.

External Assets (Community Provides)
Internal Assets (Young Person Develops)

The Central Reframe: From "Youth as Problems" to "Youth as Problem-Solvers Lacking Assets"

Karen Pittman, one of the founding theorists of the Positive Youth Development movement, articulated what has become a foundational axiom of the field: "Problem-free is not fully prepared, and fully prepared is not fully engaged." This statement captures the inadequacy of a deficit-focused approach to youth ministry and youth development. When we define young people primarily by the risk behaviors they display or the problems they present, we are committing a fundamental analytical error: we are treating the symptom as the disease.

The Villarruel, Perkins, Borden, and Keith (2003) framework for Community Youth Development (CYD) names the alternative: a paradigm in which young people are understood as potential contributors to community health, not as problems to be managed or contained. CYD is the theoretical and practical synthesis of two streams of evidence — the 40 Developmental Assets framework for individual youth development, and the Asset-Based Community Development (ABCD) approach of McKnight and Kretzmann for community-level capacity building. The synthesis holds that every community contributes to the development of its young people, and every young person can offer gifts and experiences in the process of community development and transformation.

This reframe has direct implications for how you talk about your youth, how you design your programs, and how you position young people in relation to your ministry and community. The shift from betterment to development, from doing things for youth to doing things with youth, from seeing young people as the target population to seeing them as the solution strategy — this is not rhetorical repositioning. It is an evidence-based redesign of the entire ministry enterprise.

Benson and Scales (2009) extend this reframe further with the concept of adolescent thriving — a state qualitatively distinct from merely avoiding risk. Thriving youth, their research found, are characterized by three additional constructs beyond the 40 assets: sparks (intrinsic passions and interests that ignite genuine motivation), meaningful relationships with adults who know and nurture those sparks, and genuine empowerment — the actual experience of agency and contribution. Think of Juan Mendoza noticing Danny Agosto's gift for connecting with children at play. That notice was not mere encouragement. It was the identification of a spark, the beginning of a relationship, and an offer of empowerment. It produced transformation.

What Consequential Faith Looks Like in Adolescents

The 40 Developmental Assets framework gives DVULI leaders a research tool. The Consequential Faith framework gives them a theological destination. Drawing on data from the National Study of Youth and Religion (NSYR), Kenda Creasy Dean (2010) found that most American teenagers practice what sociologist Christian Smith called Moralistic Therapeutic Deism — a vague religiosity in which God is largely distant, Christianity is primarily about being nice and feeling good, and faith makes no transformative demands on how one lives.

But Dean's research identified a small cohort of teenagers with what she termed Consequential Faith — a deeply rooted, genuinely transformative faith that orients every dimension of their lives. What distinguished these young people from their MTD peers was not more knowledge or more devotional discipline. It was the presence of four specific markers:

  • A Creed to Believe — the ability to articulate beliefs about a God who is both personal and powerful; a theology robust enough to be stated and defended
  • A Community to Belong To — relationships with adults who speak with them about issues of life and faith, not just at them; a belonging that is more than attendance
  • A Call to Live Out — a life oriented by divine vocation and service to others, not merely the pursuit of self-satisfaction
  • A Hope to Hold Onto — a sense that their lives are part of a larger story that is going somewhere good, guided by God

Notice the structural parallel between Dean's four markers and the 40 Developmental Assets. Creed maps directly onto Commitment to Learning and Positive Values — the internalization of a framework that interprets experience. Community maps onto the entire External Assets domain — the relational provision that makes belonging possible. Call maps onto Empowerment and Positive Identity — the sense of contribution and purpose that produces agency. Hope maps onto the most foundational Positive Identity asset: a positive view of personal future. A ministry that intentionally builds the 40 assets is simultaneously — and not coincidentally — building the conditions for Consequential Faith.

Dean's (2010) diagnostic of most American youth ministry is sobering: the reason teenagers practice MTD rather than consequential faith is not primarily their own theological confusion. It is, Dean argues, because that is the faith the church modeled to them. Thin theology produces thin faith. A community that cannot articulate its own creed cannot form young people in theirs. This is one reason DVULI's first eight sessions attended so carefully to the interior life of the leader — because you cannot give what you do not possess.

Think About It

Consider the four elements of Consequential Faith: Creed, Community, Call, and Hope. Which of these is your ministry most consistently providing for its young people? Which is most consistently absent? The widget below will help you examine this question systematically through the lens of your current programming.

Consequential Faith Four-Element Checker

Describe four current programs or regular activities in your ministry below. Then assign each to the Consequential Faith element it most primarily develops. The gap analysis will show which elements are underrepresented — and offer a concrete programming suggestion for each gap based on the CYD framework and urban youth ministry contexts like Jackson's DVULI cohort.

Creed — Beliefs & Theology
Community — Belonging & Relationships
Call — Vocation & Service
Hope — Purpose & Future

Implications for Your Ministry: Becoming an Asset Champion

Benson (2006) introduced the concept of the asset champion — an individual who makes the intentional building of developmental assets a consistent and visible priority in every interaction with young people. Asset champions are not primarily program directors; they are people who understand that an encouraging word to a teenager in the hallway, a deliberate conversation about future possibilities after a Sunday service, or a job responsibility handed to an adolescent who has never been trusted with one — all of these are asset-building acts. Scale, in this framework, is not primarily about program size. It is about the cumulative relational investment of adults who have decided to see young people differently.

For DVULI participants, the implications cascade in several directions simultaneously. At the individual youth level, the assets framework challenges you to rate your youth — as you did in the exercise above — and use those ratings to identify specific, targeted relational investments. At the organizational level, it challenges you to audit your programs using the DVULI Asset-Building Scorecard introduced in Local Workshop 2: Are asset building goals explicit in your mission and vision? Do your programs actively promote asset building? Are your youth partners in governance and leadership? At the community level, it challenges you to map the asset-building landscape of your neighborhood — identifying which institutions are already building which assets, where the most critical gaps exist, and how your ministry can collaborate rather than compete in filling them (Villarruel et al., 2003).

This community-level vision is precisely what the Jackson DVULI cohort attempted with their proposal for the Windsor Court Community Center. Rev. Leroy Burnett, Danny Agosto, Randall Jones, and Sandra Cestero were not simply relocating their existing programs into a renovated building. They were trying to create an integrated, neighborhood-wide asset-building ecosystem — one in which young people from the community would move along a developmental pathway from recipients to resources to assets to leaders. That pathway is the 40 assets framework made operational.

The Leader's Role: Closing the Loop from Sessions 1–8

The DVULI Personal Assessment Model introduced in Local Workshop 1 defined three dimensions of the leader's interior formation: Calling (faithfully doing the work God called you to), Character (consistently living out the values you espouse), and Commitment to Change (willingly adjusting to align with God's will). Session 9 demonstrates why all three dimensions matter for the young people in your care.

A leader who knows their calling can articulate it with conviction — and that conviction creates the kind of modeling that activates Positive Values and Positive Identity assets in youth. A leader who embodies character provides the adult role model that Asset 14 requires. A leader committed to change can build the learning orientation — the growth mindset — that activates Commitment to Learning assets. The Biblical DISC assessment you completed reveals how you naturally communicate and engage; that knowledge enables you to reach youth whose temperaments differ from yours. The Leadership Strategy Assessment (LSA) you completed identifies how you develop others; that insight equips you to match your leadership approach to where each young person is on their developmental path.

None of the interior work of Sessions 1 through 8 was separate from what you are doing in this session. It was, all along, preparation for this moment: the moment when you turn toward the young people in your community with the knowledge of who you are, the values you will live by, and a research-validated framework for understanding what they need. That is what a DVULI breakthrough looks like from the outside — not a larger budget or a newer building, but a leader who sees young people differently and acts on what they see.

Key Takeaways

  • The 40 Developmental Assets represent five decades of validated research establishing a dose-response relationship: more assets produce more thriving and fewer risk behaviors in young people, regardless of race, geography, or gender.
  • External Assets (what communities provide) and Internal Assets (what young people develop) must be cultivated simultaneously; overinvestment in either at the expense of the other produces predictable developmental failures.
  • The critical reframe of Community Youth Development shifts the analysis from individual youth moral failure to community resource deficit — a shift with direct implications for how you design programs, assign leadership roles, and communicate about youth.
  • Kenda Creasy Dean's Consequential Faith framework identifies four markers — Creed, Community, Call, and Hope — that structurally overlap with the 40 Developmental Assets, showing that the conditions for faith formation and the conditions for healthy development are, at their core, the same conditions.
  • Caring adults outside the family — including faith community leaders and mentors — are among the most powerful asset-building forces available to youth in under-resourced communities, with especially significant impact where family assets are limited.
  • The interior formation work of Sessions 1–8 was not preliminary to your community ministry; it was foundational to it. Healthy leaders are the proximate cause of healthy asset-building environments for youth.

Looking Ahead

Session 10 will extend the outward pivot begun here by introducing the tools of Community Capacity Building — the Asset-Based Community Development framework of McKnight and Kretzmann, community asset mapping, and the principles of building from the inside out. You will take your ratings from the 40 Assets exercise today and connect them to the neighborhood asset map you will construct with your youth, identifying the organizational, associational, and institutional assets in your community that can collectively produce the developmental ecosystem your young people need.

blocks.title: Key Concepts
concepts: 40 Developmental Assets: the eight external and internal asset categories and their research-based relationship to adolescent thriving
External vs. Internal Assets: what communities provide versus what young people develop
Consequential Faith: creed, community, call, and hope as the four marks of adolescents with deeply rooted faith (Kenda Creasy Dean)
Reframing youth: from 'youth as problems' to 'youth as problem-solvers lacking assets'

items.question: The 40 Developmental Assets framework was developed by which organization based on five decades of research into adolescent thriving?
items.correctFeedback: The Search Institute of Minneapolis developed the 40 Developmental Assets framework through five decades of research into what building blocks young people need to grow up healthy, make wise choices, and become contributing community members.

distractors.text: The National Institute of Child Health and Human Development
distractors.feedback: Students may confuse the Search Institute with other well-known research organizations focused on youth. The Search Institute is a distinct organization specifically known for the Developmental Assets framework.

distractors.text: The Center for Disease Control's Division of Adolescent Health
distractors.feedback: While the CDC does conduct youth health research, the 40 Developmental Assets framework is the specific product of the Search Institute of Minneapolis, which focuses on positive youth development rather than disease prevention.

distractors.text: The DeVos Urban Leadership Initiative (DVULI)
distractors.feedback: DVULI uses the 40 Developmental Assets framework as a lens for training urban youth leaders, but the framework itself was developed by the Search Institute of Minneapolis. DVULI is the training program, not the research organization.

items.question: Which of the following is an example of an External Asset category in the 40 Developmental Assets framework?
items.correctFeedback: Boundaries and Expectations is one of the four External Asset categories (along with Support, Empowerment, and Constructive Use of Time). External Assets are the things that communities, families, and organizations provide to young people from the outside.

distractors.text: Positive Identity
distractors.feedback: Positive Identity is an Internal Asset category, not an External one. Internal Assets are strengths that young people develop from within, whereas External Assets are provided by communities, families, and organizations.

distractors.text: Social Competencies
distractors.feedback: Social Competencies is an Internal Asset category. Although social skills are developed in community contexts, the framework classifies them as internal strengths that young people cultivate within themselves.

distractors.text: Commitment to Learning
distractors.feedback: Commitment to Learning is an Internal Asset category. While learning environments are external, the commitment itself is an internally developed strength according to the framework's classification.

items.question: According to the Developmental Assets research, what is the threshold number of assets associated with positive development in young people?
items.correctFeedback: The research identifies 31 assets as the threshold for positive development. The sobering finding is that most young people in under-resourced urban communities have far fewer than this threshold, which helps explain the prevalence of risky behaviors in those settings.

distractors.text: 20 assets
distractors.feedback: Students may underestimate the threshold. The research actually identifies 31 out of 40 assets as the threshold, which is notably high and underscores how many building blocks are needed for young people to thrive.

distractors.text: 40 assets
distractors.feedback: While having all 40 assets would be ideal, the research identifies 31 as the threshold for positive development. It would be unrealistic to require every single asset for a young person to be on a positive trajectory.

distractors.text: 25 assets
distractors.feedback: This is a reasonable-sounding estimate but is incorrect. The threshold identified by the research is 31 assets, which is higher than many might expect and highlights the significant investment communities must make in young people.

items.question: In Kenda Creasy Dean's Consequential Faith framework from Almost Christian, which of the following is one of the four marks of adolescents with deeply rooted faith?
items.correctFeedback: The four marks of Consequential Faith according to Kenda Creasy Dean are: a creed to believe, a community to belong to, a call to live out, and a hope to hold onto. A sense of calling gives young people purpose and direction rooted in their faith.

distractors.text: A curriculum to study
distractors.feedback: While education is important, Dean's framework does not list curriculum as a mark of consequential faith. The four marks are creed, community, call, and hope — emphasizing belief, belonging, purpose, and future orientation rather than formal study.

distractors.text: A church to attend
distractors.feedback: Dean's framework includes 'a community to belong to' rather than 'a church to attend.' This distinction is important because belonging implies relational depth and identity formation, whereas attendance alone is a passive activity.

distractors.text: A crisis to overcome
distractors.feedback: While adversity can shape faith, Dean's framework identifies hope as the forward-looking mark, not crisis. The four marks — creed, community, call, and hope — are all constructive building blocks, not obstacles to overcome.

items.question: What is the central conceptual shift that Community Youth Development demands, according to this session?
items.correctAnswer: From viewing youth as problems to viewing youth as problem-solvers lacking assets
items.correctFeedback: This reframe is the core conceptual shift of Community Youth Development. It recognizes that struggling youth are not morally deficient but rather lack the relational, organizational, and community resources that produce developmental assets. The deficit lies in the environment, not the young person.

distractors.text: From viewing youth as passive recipients to viewing youth as independent self-starters
distractors.feedback: While this captures some of the spirit of empowerment, it misses the key insight about assets. The framework emphasizes that youth need resources and support systems, not that they should be expected to thrive independently without them.

distractors.text: From viewing youth as academically deficient to viewing youth as spiritually mature
distractors.feedback: This answer creates a false dichotomy between academics and spirituality. The actual reframe is broader — it shifts blame from the young person's character to the absence of developmental assets in their environment.

distractors.text: From viewing youth as at-risk to viewing youth as resilient enough to succeed without intervention
distractors.feedback: This distorts the reframe by suggesting youth don't need support. The correct reframe acknowledges that youth are capable problem-solvers who still require the relational and community assets that the framework identifies.

items.question: In the asset-rating exercise, participants rate three representative youth from their ministries. What profound insight does this exercise typically produce?
items.correctAnswer: The difference between thriving and struggling youth is usually a deficit of resources, not a moral failure of the struggling youth
items.correctFeedback: The rating exercise reveals that the gap between thriving youth and struggling youth is not a matter of character or moral superiority but rather a difference in the relational, organizational, and community resources available to each. This is the foundation of the asset-based reframe.

distractors.text: Thriving youth consistently have stronger family structures than struggling youth
distractors.feedback: While family support is one External Asset category, this answer is too narrow. The exercise reveals that the full range of assets — not just family structure — accounts for the difference, and it emphasizes systemic resource deficits rather than singling out one factor.

distractors.text: Most youth in urban ministries already exceed the threshold of assets needed for positive development
distractors.feedback: The research finding is actually the opposite: most young people in under-resourced urban communities have far fewer than the 31 assets that represent the positive development threshold. This is described as a sobering finding.

distractors.text: Struggling youth are primarily distinguished from thriving youth by their level of church attendance
distractors.feedback: Church attendance alone is far too narrow an explanation. The exercise demonstrates that the difference spans all 40 assets across eight categories — including support, empowerment, values, identity, and more — not a single behavioral measure.

items.question: Why does Session 9 represent a deliberate pivot in the DVULI curriculum?
items.correctAnswer: It shifts focus from the leader's interior life to the young people and communities they serve
items.correctFeedback: Sessions 1–8 focused on the leader's self-development, Core Values, and interior life. Session 9 pivots outward to focus on youth and communities. DVULI sees this not as a departure but as the destination of the earlier work — healthy leaders build healthy organizations that serve healthy communities.

distractors.text: It introduces the first faith-based content into a previously secular curriculum
distractors.feedback: The DVULI curriculum is not divided into secular and faith-based sections. Earlier sessions included spiritual formation and Core Values work. Session 9's pivot is about moving from self-focus to community focus, not from secular to sacred content.

distractors.text: It marks the transition from theoretical learning to field-based practicum work
distractors.feedback: While Session 9 does include a practical exercise (rating youth across the 40 assets), the pivot is specifically described as moving from the leader's interior life outward to the youth and communities they serve, not a shift from theory to practicum.

distractors.text: It replaces individual reflection with group-based peer accountability structures
distractors.feedback: The pivot is not about changing the pedagogical method from individual to group work. Rather, it shifts the subject of attention from the leader's own development to understanding the developmental needs of the youth they serve.

items.question: How does the Consequential Faith framework connect to the 40 Developmental Assets model?
items.correctAnswer: Both frameworks identify environmental and relational building blocks necessary for adolescent thriving, applied in complementary domains
items.correctFeedback: The Developmental Assets framework identifies what young people need to thrive developmentally, while the Consequential Faith framework identifies what they need to thrive spiritually — creed, community, call, and hope. Both reject the idea that adolescent outcomes are simply about individual choices and instead emphasize the relational and environmental conditions that produce thriving.

distractors.text: Consequential Faith replaces the Developmental Assets framework with a theologically superior model that has been validated across 50+ denominational contexts
distractors.feedback: The two frameworks are presented as complementary, not competing. The session uses both together — the Developmental Assets framework provides the broader developmental lens while Consequential Faith adds the specifically faith-related dimensions.

distractors.text: The Developmental Assets framework was derived directly from Dean's research on Consequential Faith
distractors.feedback: These are independently developed frameworks from different researchers and organizations. The Search Institute developed the 40 Developmental Assets, while Kenda Creasy Dean developed the Consequential Faith framework. The DVULI curriculum connects them, but one was not derived from the other.

distractors.text: Consequential Faith focuses exclusively on Internal Assets while the Developmental Assets framework focuses only on External Assets
distractors.feedback: This creates a false division. The 40 Developmental Assets framework includes both External and Internal Asset categories. And Consequential Faith includes community (an external element) alongside creed, call, and hope. Neither framework is limited to one dimension.

items.question: DVULI's conviction that 'healthy leaders build healthy organizations that serve healthy communities' implies that which link in this chain is most directly addressed by Sessions 1–8?
items.correctFeedback: Sessions 1–8 focused on the leader's interior life, self-awareness, and Core Values — all aimed at developing healthy leaders. Session 9 then pivots to the community and youth focus. DVULI's conviction is that this chain cannot be skipped; leader health is the prerequisite foundation for organizational and community health.

distractors.text: Healthy organizations
distractors.feedback: Organizational health is part of the chain but is not the primary focus of Sessions 1–8. Those sessions concentrate on the leader's personal development and interior life. Organizational applications flow from leader health but are addressed later in the curriculum.

distractors.text: Healthy communities
distractors.feedback: Community health is the ultimate destination of the chain, but Sessions 1–8 focus on the starting point: the leader's own health and development. Session 9 begins the pivot toward community, illustrating DVULI's conviction that the chain cannot be skipped.

distractors.text: All three links equally
distractors.feedback: While all three links are important to DVULI's mission, Sessions 1–8 specifically concentrated on the leader's interior life, Core Values, and personal formation. The curriculum is sequenced intentionally so that leader health comes first before addressing organizational and community health.

items.question: A youth leader notices that a young person in their program has strong Internal Assets (commitment to learning, positive values) but weak External Assets (limited family support, few structured activities). Based on the Developmental Assets framework, what is the most accurate interpretation of this young person's situation?
items.correctAnswer: The young person is at risk because the absence of External Assets may eventually erode the Internal Assets they have developed
items.correctFeedback: The Developmental Assets framework emphasizes that more assets correlate with greater thriving and fewer risky behaviors. External and Internal Assets work together — communities, families, and organizations provide the external scaffolding that reinforces and sustains the internal strengths young people develop. As you move into Chapter 10, notice how this same logic appears in ABCD methodology: what the Assets framework calls external support, ABCD calls community gifts and associational life. The frameworks share more ground than they first appear to.

distractors.text: The young person is thriving because Internal Assets are ultimately more important than External Assets in the framework
distractors.feedback: The framework does not rank Internal Assets as more important than External Assets. Both categories are essential, and the research shows that the total number of assets — across all eight categories — is what predicts outcomes. Strong Internal Assets alone do not guarantee thriving.

distractors.text: The young person demonstrates that moral character can fully compensate for community resource deficits
distractors.feedback: This interpretation contradicts the session's central reframe. The framework specifically argues against reducing outcomes to individual moral character, emphasizing instead that environmental and relational resources are necessary for sustained positive development.

distractors.text: The youth leader should focus exclusively on building the young person's External Assets and stop developing Internal Assets since those are already strong
distractors.feedback: The framework does not recommend neglecting any asset category. Both External and Internal Assets are necessary, and maintaining Internal Assets requires ongoing investment even as External Assets are being built. The goal is to increase the total number of assets across all categories.

questions.stem: In the Search Institute's 40 Developmental Assets framework, which of the following is one of the four External Asset categories?
options: Boundaries and Expectations
Social Competencies
Positive Identity
Commitment to Learning

feedback.correct: Boundaries and Expectations is one of the four External Asset categories — the things that communities, families, and organizations provide to young people. The other three external categories are Support, Empowerment, and Constructive Use of Time.
feedback.incorrect: Social Competencies, Positive Identity, and Commitment to Learning are all Internal Asset categories — strengths that young people develop from within. The four External Asset categories are Support, Empowerment, Boundaries and Expectations, and Constructive Use of Time.

variants.stem: Which of the following is classified as an Internal Asset category in the 40 Developmental Assets framework?
options: Positive Values
Support
Empowerment
Constructive Use of Time

feedback.correct: Positive Values is one of the four Internal Asset categories — strengths that young people develop from within. The other internal categories are Commitment to Learning, Social Competencies, and Positive Identity.
feedback.incorrect: Support, Empowerment, and Constructive Use of Time are all External Asset categories — things that communities, families, and organizations provide. The four Internal Asset categories are Commitment to Learning, Positive Values, Social Competencies, and Positive Identity.

questions.stem: According to Kenda Creasy Dean's Consequential Faith framework, what are the four marks of adolescents with deeply rooted faith?
options: Creed, community, call, and hope
Belief, baptism, belonging, and behavior
Prayer, service, worship, and study
Faith, love, obedience, and perseverance

feedback.correct: Dean identifies creed (something to believe), community (somewhere to belong), call (something to live out), and hope (something to hold onto) as the four marks that distinguish adolescents whose faith is consequential — faith that actually shapes their lives and choices.
feedback.incorrect: While the other options describe important aspects of Christian life, Dean's specific framework from Almost Christian identifies creed, community, call, and hope as the four distinguishing marks. Each addresses a different dimension of faith that moves it from nominal to consequential.

variants.stem: In Kenda Creasy Dean's framework from Almost Christian, which combination correctly identifies what thriving adolescents of faith possess?
options: A creed to believe, a community to belong to, a call to live out, and a hope to hold onto
A church to attend, a Bible to study, a mentor to follow, and a mission to complete
A doctrine to defend, a tradition to uphold, a vocation to pursue, and a reward to earn
A prayer life to maintain, a testimony to share, a discipline to keep, and a future to plan

feedback.correct: Dean's Consequential Faith framework emphasizes four marks: creed (belief), community (belonging), call (purpose), and hope (future orientation). These four together distinguish youth whose faith deeply shapes their identity and behavior.
feedback.incorrect: Dean's research specifically identifies creed, community, call, and hope — not general spiritual practices. The distinction matters because Consequential Faith is about the structural conditions that make faith formative, not just the activities youth engage in.

questions.stem: A youth leader rates three teens in her ministry using the 40 Developmental Assets. She finds that the teen displaying multiple risk behaviors scores low primarily on external assets like Support and Empowerment, while scoring moderately on internal assets like Positive Values. What does this pattern most clearly demonstrate?
options: The struggling teen lacks relational and community resources, not personal morality
The struggling teen needs more discipline and clearer moral instruction
Internal assets are more important than external assets for preventing risk behaviors
The 40 Developmental Assets framework cannot distinguish between thriving and struggling youth
justifications: The asset rating exercise reveals that the difference between thriving and struggling youth is typically a deficit of external resources rather than a moral failure, supporting the reframe to 'youth as problem-solvers lacking assets'
Youth with moderate positive values but low external support need stronger internal convictions to overcome their environmental challenges
External assets are less important because internal motivation is what ultimately drives behavior change in adolescents
The pattern shows the framework works best for identifying internal deficits rather than external ones

feedback.correct: This is the central insight of the DVULI rating exercise: when struggling youth score low on external assets but moderate on internal ones, it reveals that the problem is not the young person's character but the absence of community, family, and organizational support systems. This supports the paradigm shift from 'youth as problems' to 'youth as problem-solvers lacking resources.'
feedback.incorrect: The pattern of low external assets and moderate internal assets contradicts the idea that struggling youth need more moral instruction. The youth already has positive values — what's missing is the relational infrastructure that communities provide. This is the core reframe of Community Youth Development.
feedback.wrongReason: Students sometimes correctly identify the resource deficit but then justify it by saying internal assets matter more, which actually undermines the framework's emphasis on the interplay between external supports and internal development.

variants.stem: During the DVULI asset-rating exercise, a participant discovers that a consistently engaged teen in his ministry scores high on both Support and Empowerment (external) and Commitment to Learning and Positive Identity (internal), while a teen who recently dropped out scores very low on external assets but has moderate internal assets. What does this comparison most clearly illustrate?
options: The gap between the two teens is primarily explained by differences in community-provided resources, not personal character
The engaged teen is simply more disciplined and motivated than the teen who dropped out
External assets only matter for youth who already have strong internal assets
The framework shows that some youth are naturally more resilient than others
justifications: The contrast in external asset scores — while internal assets are comparable — demonstrates that community-provided resources like support and empowerment are what differentiate thriving from struggling youth, confirming the 'youth as problem-solvers lacking assets' reframe
The higher external assets of the engaged teen prove that youth who seek out support are inherently more likely to succeed
This shows that internal assets are only activated when sufficient external assets are present, making external assets the sole predictor of thriving
The comparison proves that asset ratings are better at identifying strengths than explaining deficits

feedback.correct: The side-by-side comparison makes the reframe vivid: both teens have comparable internal assets (values, commitment), but the thriving teen has the community-provided external supports that the struggling teen lacks. The difference is not character but context — which is the foundational insight of Community Youth Development.
feedback.incorrect: Attributing the difference to the engaged teen's discipline or natural resilience falls back into the 'youth as problem' mindset. The asset framework is designed to show that environmental resources, not individual traits, are the primary differentiator.
feedback.wrongReason: Some students correctly see the resource gap but then over-conclude that external assets are the SOLE factor, ignoring the framework's emphasis on the interplay between external and internal assets.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: The more developmental assets a young person possesses, the more likely they are to thrive and the less likely they are to engage in risky behaviors.
questions.reason: The 40 Developmental Assets represent five decades of Search Institute research into the building blocks necessary for healthy youth development.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion. The research base (five decades of study) establishes the credibility of the framework, but it does not explain WHY more assets lead to thriving. The explanation for that relationship lies in how external supports and internal strengths work together to create resilience and healthy decision-making — not in the duration of the research program.
feedback.incorrect: A common error is assuming that because the research discovered the relationship, the research itself explains it. The length and rigor of the research validates the finding, but the causal mechanism — how assets produce thriving — is a separate matter from the evidence base that documented it.

variants.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
variants.assertion: Most young people in under-resourced urban communities have far fewer than the 31 developmental assets that represent the threshold for positive development.
variants.reason: Under-resourced communities often lack the institutional infrastructure — strong schools, safe recreational spaces, mentoring networks — needed to provide external assets like Support, Empowerment, and Constructive Use of Time.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the reason directly explains the assertion. The deficit of developmental assets in under-resourced communities is caused by the lack of institutional infrastructure that provides external assets. When communities lack strong schools, safe spaces, and mentoring networks, the external asset categories (Support, Empowerment, Boundaries and Expectations, Constructive Use of Time) cannot be adequately supplied.
feedback.incorrect: Some students mark these as true-but-independent, not seeing the causal link. The institutional infrastructure described in the reason is precisely the mechanism through which external assets are delivered. Without those structures, the asset deficit described in the assertion is a direct and predictable consequence.

questions.stem: The 40 Developmental Assets framework divides assets into how many total categories across the external and internal domains?
options: Eight categories (four external, four internal)
Six categories (three external, three internal)
Ten categories (five external, five internal)
Four categories (two external, two internal)

feedback.correct: The framework includes eight total categories. The four External Asset categories are Support, Empowerment, Boundaries and Expectations, and Constructive Use of Time. The four Internal Asset categories are Commitment to Learning, Positive Values, Social Competencies, and Positive Identity.
feedback.incorrect: The framework is organized into exactly eight categories — four external and four internal. Remembering the symmetry (4 + 4 = 8) can help anchor the structure. The external categories address what communities provide; the internal categories address what young people develop within themselves.

variants.stem: How many of the eight Developmental Asset categories are classified as External Assets — things that communities, families, and organizations provide to young people?
options: Four: Support, Empowerment, Boundaries and Expectations, and Constructive Use of Time
Three: Support, Empowerment, and Boundaries and Expectations
Five: Support, Empowerment, Boundaries and Expectations, Constructive Use of Time, and Positive Values
Two: Support and Empowerment

feedback.correct: Four categories are external: Support, Empowerment, Boundaries and Expectations, and Constructive Use of Time. These represent what communities, families, and organizations provide. The remaining four categories — Commitment to Learning, Positive Values, Social Competencies, and Positive Identity — are internal.
feedback.incorrect: A common mistake is misclassifying Positive Values as external (since communities teach values) or forgetting Constructive Use of Time as external. The framework places all value-related and competency-related assets in the internal domain, while Constructive Use of Time is external because it depends on community-provided opportunities and programs.

questions.stem: Classify each statement about the 40 Developmental Assets framework as Always True, Sometimes True, or Never True:

statements.text: External assets are things that communities, families, and organizations provide to young people.
Accumulating more developmental assets reduces a young person's likelihood of engaging in risky behavior.
A young person who is struggling with risky behaviors is demonstrating a personal moral failure.
Positive Identity is classified as an external asset category.
Youth in under-resourced urban communities have fewer than 31 developmental assets.
A single youth ministry program can supply all 40 developmental assets for a young person.
Boundaries and Expectations is classified as an external asset category.

feedback.correct: The key distinctions here involve understanding which assets are external versus internal (provided by community vs. developed from within), recognizing the probabilistic rather than deterministic nature of the asset-behavior relationship, and embracing the reframe from moral failure to resource deficit. The framework is about what communities provide, not about labeling individual youth.
feedback.incorrect: Common confusions include misclassifying asset categories (Positive Identity sounds like it could be external, but it's internal; Boundaries and Expectations sounds internal, but it's external because communities set them) and treating the asset-risk relationship as absolute rather than probabilistic. Also, the 'most youth in under-resourced communities' finding is a general trend, not a universal — some youth in these communities do exceed the threshold.

variants.stem: Classify each statement about the 40 Developmental Assets framework and Consequential Faith as Always True, Sometimes True, or Never True:

statements.text: Internal assets are strengths that young people develop from within themselves.
A young person with all four marks of Consequential Faith (creed, community, call, hope) will never engage in risky behavior.
Constructive Use of Time is classified as an internal asset category.
The difference between a thriving youth and a struggling youth is explained by differences in available resources and assets.
Support is classified as an external asset category.
Youth who lack developmental assets are best understood as problem-solvers who lack resources.
Having 31 or more developmental assets guarantees positive development outcomes for a young person.

feedback.correct: These distinctions test whether you understand the probabilistic nature of the asset framework (more assets = more likely to thrive, but never guaranteed), the external/internal classification system, and the reframe lens. The 31-asset threshold represents increased likelihood, not certainty, and resource deficits explain much — but not always all — of the difference between thriving and struggling youth.
feedback.incorrect: The most common errors involve treating probabilistic relationships as certainties. The asset framework identifies trends and likelihoods, not guarantees. Also, while the resource-deficit explanation is the 'central conceptual shift' of the framework, individual cases can be more complex, which is why the thriving-struggling difference is 'sometimes' rather than 'always' fully explained by resources alone.

questions.stem: According to Search Institute research, how many of the 40 developmental assets represent the threshold number needed for positive youth development?

feedback.correct: The threshold is 31 out of 40 developmental assets. This is a notably high number — over three-quarters of all assets — and helps explain why the research finding is described as 'sobering.' Most young people in under-resourced urban communities fall far below this threshold, meaning they lack the building blocks the research shows are necessary for healthy development.
feedback.incorrect: Many people underestimate this threshold, guessing somewhere around 20-25 assets. The actual threshold of 31 (about 78% of all 40 assets) underscores how many building blocks are needed for positive development and why comprehensive, community-wide approaches — not just individual programs — are necessary.

variants.stem: Approximately what percentage of the 40 developmental assets does the Search Institute identify as the threshold needed for positive youth development?

feedback.correct: The threshold is approximately 78% (31 out of 40 assets). This high percentage reveals why single programs or individual relationships, while valuable, are insufficient. Reaching the threshold requires coordinated effort across families, schools, congregations, and community organizations — each contributing different categories of assets.
feedback.incorrect: Students often estimate around 50-60%, assuming a simple majority of assets would suffice. The actual threshold of about 78% highlights the comprehensiveness required. Young people need support across nearly all asset categories — external and internal — to reach the level associated with positive development outcomes.

questions.stem: Desiree, a DVULI participant, leads a youth ministry in an under-resourced neighborhood. She rates 14-year-old Kayla using the 40 Developmental Assets and finds: Kayla scores high on Commitment to Learning and Positive Values (internal assets) but very low on Support, Empowerment, and Constructive Use of Time (external assets). Kayla scores low on Positive Identity and recently told Desiree, 'I go to church but I don't really know what I believe or why it matters. I don't see how any of this connects to my life.' Desiree's instinct is to lecture Kayla about making better choices and staying committed to the youth group. Using the frameworks from this session, what should Desiree do instead?
options: Build Kayla's missing external assets (support, empowerment, constructive time) while addressing her faith formation through creed, call, and hope from the Consequential Faith framework
Focus on strengthening Kayla's existing internal assets since those are the foundation that external assets build upon
Prioritize getting Kayla more involved in church activities since increased participation will naturally develop all missing assets
Address Kayla's Positive Identity deficit first since internal assets must be built before external assets can take hold
justifications: Kayla's asset profile shows an external resource deficit, not a moral failure — she already has strong values and learning commitment. Her statement about faith reveals she lacks creed (what to believe), call (connection to her life), and hope, which are three of Dean's four marks of Consequential Faith. Addressing both the external asset gap and the faith formation gap treats Kayla as a problem-solver lacking resources.
Since Kayla's internal assets are already strong, the most effective strategy is to build on that existing foundation by deepening her values and commitment before adding external supports
Regular church involvement is the most comprehensive asset-builder because it simultaneously addresses support, community, empowerment, and faith development in a single setting
Positive Identity is the most critical asset category because without a strong sense of self, external supports cannot be effectively received or utilized by the young person

feedback.correct: This scenario integrates three key frameworks: (1) the 40 Developmental Assets — Kayla's low external assets reveal a resource gap, not a character flaw; (2) the reframe — Desiree must shift from lecturing about choices to providing the community supports Kayla lacks; and (3) Consequential Faith — Kayla's own words reveal she lacks creed ('don't know what I believe'), call ('don't see how this connects to my life'), and hope. The correct approach addresses the external asset deficit AND the faith formation gap simultaneously, treating Kayla as a capable young person who needs resources, not reprimand.
feedback.incorrect: Focusing only on internal assets, church attendance, or Positive Identity addresses just one dimension of Kayla's situation. The asset rating clearly shows external assets are the primary gap, and Kayla's statement maps directly onto Dean's Consequential Faith marks. Lecturing or increasing church attendance treats symptoms rather than root causes — and falls back into the 'youth as problem' mindset that this session challenges.
feedback.wrongReason: A student might correctly choose the comprehensive approach but justify it by saying internal assets need to be built first or that church attendance covers everything. The correct reasoning must connect Kayla's specific external asset deficits to the community's responsibility AND connect her faith statement to the specific marks of Consequential Faith (creed, call, hope) — demonstrating integration of multiple frameworks rather than reliance on a single concept.

variants.stem: Tony, a DVULI participant, works with 15-year-old Devon, who has recently been suspended from school for fighting. Tony's initial impulse is to discipline Devon and set firmer boundaries. However, after applying the 40 Developmental Assets framework, Tony discovers Devon scores very low on Support and Empowerment (external assets) and low on Social Competencies and Positive Identity (internal assets), but moderately high on Positive Values and Commitment to Learning. Devon has told Tony, 'Nobody at church actually cares about me — I just show up because my grandma makes me. I don't even know what I'm supposed to be doing with my life.' Which approach best reflects the session's frameworks?
options: Address Devon's external asset deficits by mobilizing community support and empowerment while using the Consequential Faith framework to develop his sense of community, call, and hope
Focus primarily on building Devon's Social Competencies since his fighting indicates he lacks conflict resolution skills
Set clearer boundaries and expectations for Devon since that external asset category directly addresses behavioral problems like fighting
Strengthen Devon's existing Positive Values and Commitment to Learning as the foundation for addressing all other deficits
justifications: Devon's asset profile reveals deficits in community-provided resources (Support, Empowerment) and his statement maps directly onto Consequential Faith gaps — he lacks genuine community ('nobody actually cares'), call ('don't know what I'm supposed to be doing'), and hope. The reframe from 'youth as problem' to 'youth as problem-solver lacking assets' means addressing the resource and relational deficits rather than punishing the behavior they produce.
Social Competencies training is the evidence-based response to aggressive behavior, and once Devon develops better conflict resolution skills, other asset areas will improve naturally
Boundaries and Expectations is the external asset category most directly related to school suspension, and strengthening this category addresses the root behavioral issue
Devon's moderate Positive Values and Commitment to Learning prove he has the internal foundation needed, so building on existing strengths is more effective than addressing deficits

feedback.correct: Tony's situation requires integrating all three frameworks: (1) Devon's asset profile shows the fighting is connected to a broader external resource deficit, not just a skills gap; (2) the reframe shifts from punishing Devon's behavior to providing what his community has failed to provide; and (3) Devon's own words reveal he lacks community ('nobody cares'), call ('don't know what I'm supposed to be doing'), and hope — three of Dean's four Consequential Faith marks. The comprehensive approach addresses root causes across frameworks.
feedback.incorrect: Targeting just one asset category (Social Competencies, Boundaries and Expectations, or existing strengths) treats one symptom while ignoring the interconnected web of deficits. Devon's situation requires the 'youth as problem-solver lacking resources' reframe combined with attention to his Consequential Faith gaps — approaches that address the systemic resource deficit rather than the individual behavioral symptom.
feedback.wrongReason: Students may correctly select the comprehensive approach but justify it by focusing on only one framework (e.g., only the asset deficit or only Consequential Faith). The correct reasoning must explicitly connect Devon's external asset gaps to the reframe AND map his words onto the specific Consequential Faith marks — demonstrating that the frameworks work together, not in isolation.

questions.stem: The Five Core Values framework (Week 5) emphasizes a specific relationship between 'being' and 'doing' in ministry leadership. Which statement best captures that relationship?
options: Being one's best is the necessary foundation for doing one's best
Doing one's best in ministry naturally produces the character growth of being one's best
Being and doing are equally important but independent dimensions of leadership
Doing is prioritized over being because ministry outcomes are the ultimate measure of leadership

feedback.correct: The Core Values framework insists that being precedes doing — the leader's interior character, formed through accountability, balance, interdependence, empowerment, and leverage, is the necessary foundation for effective ministry action. This is why DVULI devotes the first eight sessions to the leader's inner life before pivoting to community-facing content in Session 9.
feedback.incorrect: A common ministry temptation is to prioritize doing (programs, events, outcomes) over being (character, values, self-awareness). The Core Values framework explicitly reverses this, arguing that sustainable ministry effectiveness flows from who the leader is, not merely what the leader does. Being and doing are not independent — being is the foundation on which doing rests.

variants.stem: In the DVULI curriculum, why do Sessions 1–8 focus on the leader's interior life before Session 9 pivots to community-facing content? Which Core Values principle best explains this sequence?
options: Being one's best must precede doing one's best — leaders must develop internally before they can effectively serve externally
Leaders need to master all five Core Values individually before they can see how the values work as an interconnected system
The curriculum reserves community-facing content for later so leaders are not overwhelmed by too many frameworks at once
Sessions 1–8 address the leader's weaknesses while Session 9 begins addressing the community's weaknesses

feedback.correct: DVULI's conviction is that healthy leaders build healthy organizations that serve healthy communities — and the chain cannot be skipped. The 'being before doing' principle from the Five Core Values explains why eight sessions of interior development must precede the outward pivot. Leaders cannot offer what they have not cultivated within themselves.
feedback.incorrect: The sequencing is not about pacing or separating weaknesses — it reflects the foundational DVULI conviction that inner formation is the prerequisite for outer effectiveness. The curriculum is designed so that the Core Values work of being one's best makes the community-facing work of doing one's best possible.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: Empowerment in ministry leadership means developing leader-full organizations rather than leader-dependent ones.
questions.reason: The Action Hero problem describes how a leader's individual performance instincts can undermine collective ministry capacity.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the reason directly explains the assertion. The Action Hero problem — where leaders rely on their own abilities rather than equipping others — is precisely the dysfunction that empowerment addresses. When leaders act as solo performers, they create leader-dependent organizations. The empowerment framework (Identifying → Equipping → Mobilizing → Supporting) deliberately counters the Action Hero instinct by distributing leadership capacity across the organization.
feedback.incorrect: Students sometimes see these as true-but-independent, thinking the Action Hero problem is about ego while empowerment is about strategy. In fact, they are causally connected: the Action Hero problem is the diagnosis, and empowerment (leader-full organizations) is the prescription. The reason explains WHY the assertion's approach is necessary.

variants.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
variants.assertion: The Interdependence core value teaches that diverse gifts in genuine community produce results no individual can achieve alone.
variants.reason: The empowerment process follows a sequence of Identifying, Equipping, Mobilizing, and Supporting emerging leaders.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion. Interdependence is grounded in the theological principle of the body of Christ (1 Corinthians 12) and collaborative intelligence — the idea that diversity produces superior collective outcomes. The empowerment process is a separate operational framework for developing leaders. While they complement each other in practice, the empowerment sequence does not explain WHY diverse gifts produce results no individual can achieve.
feedback.incorrect: These concepts are related in that both appear in Week 7 and both value collective capacity over individual performance. However, having a relationship is different from one explaining the other. Interdependence is a theological and systemic principle; the empowerment process is a practical leadership development methodology. They are both true but causally independent.

questions.stem: The Agenda for Change framework from Week 5 provides a three-step process for translating personal insight into committed action. What is the correct sequence of those three steps?
options: Facing Truth → Committing to Change → Taking Charge
Taking Charge → Facing Truth → Committing to Change
Committing to Change → Taking Charge → Facing Truth
Facing Truth → Taking Charge → Committing to Change

feedback.correct: The Agenda for Change follows a deliberate sequence: first, Facing Truth (honestly acknowledging reality), then Committing to Change (making a personal decision to act differently), and finally Taking Charge (implementing the change with intentional action). The order matters because commitment without truth-facing is superficial, and action without commitment is unsustainable.
feedback.incorrect: The sequence begins with Facing Truth because genuine change requires honest self-assessment before commitment or action can be meaningful. Students sometimes place Taking Charge earlier because action feels urgent, but the framework insists that truth and commitment must precede action for the change to be lasting.

variants.stem: A DVULI participant realizes she has been avoiding honest conversations with her team about the ministry's declining effectiveness. According to the Agenda for Change framework, which step must she engage in first?
options: Facing Truth — honestly acknowledging the reality of the ministry's decline before committing to any course of action
Taking Charge — immediately implementing structural changes to reverse the decline
Committing to Change — deciding that she will prioritize hard conversations going forward
All three steps should be engaged simultaneously for maximum impact

feedback.correct: Facing Truth is always the first step in the Agenda for Change. The participant must honestly acknowledge the reality — including her own avoidance — before she can meaningfully commit to change or take charge of the situation. Skipping to commitment or action without facing truth results in superficial change that rarely lasts.
feedback.incorrect: While the instinct to take immediate action or make a commitment is understandable, the Agenda for Change framework insists on a specific sequence. Facing Truth comes first because without an honest reckoning with reality, any subsequent commitment or action will be built on a distorted foundation.

prompts.prompt: Who made the biggest difference in your life growing up — a parent, a teacher, a coach, a neighbour, a friend? What did they actually DO that mattered?
Hot take: most 'troubled teenagers' aren't broken — they're just missing the right support. Do you buy that, or is it too easy an excuse?
Would you rather grow up in a neighbourhood where everyone knows you, checks on you, and has high expectations for you — OR have total freedom where adults mostly leave you alone?
Did you turn out the way you did mostly because of the people and community around you — or because of who you are on the inside? Be honest.

Think about a teenager you know (or remember being one). Is your first instinct to see them as a problem to manage — or a person who just needs the right opportunities? Where does that instinct come from?
Does having a religion, a faith community, or even just a strong sense of purpose actually make teens more grounded — or does it just give them something to rebel against?

activities.title: My Asset Inventory: Then and Now
activities.description: Students open a shared digital response form (Google Form, course discussion board, or app poll) and are given a curated list of 10-12 of the 40 Developmental Assets drawn from each category. They privately rate each one on a simple scale: 'I had this as a teenager / I partially had this / I rarely experienced this.' After completing the inventory, students are prompted to post two responses in the discussion thread: (1) one asset they are grateful they had and why, and (2) one asset they lacked and how they think it affected their adolescence. The instructor then posts a summary of the group's patterns — what was commonly present and what was commonly missing — and invites students to notice what that reveals about the communities they came from. This grounds the research in lived experience before moving to theory.
activities.materials: Digital response form or discussion board thread pre-built in the course app; curated 10-12 asset list formatted for easy reading on mobile/desktop
