activities.learningGoal: Personalizes the 40 Assets framework so students see it as descriptive of real developmental experience, not just abstract research
activities.scalingNotes: Works well at any size asynchronously. For groups larger than 40, consider splitting into sub-threads by asset category to keep discussion readable.

activities.title: External or Internal? Asset Sorting Challenge
activities.description: The instructor pre-builds a digital drag-and-drop sorting activity (using a tool like Padlet, Mentimeter, or a purpose-built card-sort in the course LMS) that presents 16 randomly ordered asset examples — some external, some internal — without labels. Students sort them into two columns: 'Something a community provides' versus 'Something a young person grows inside themselves.' After students submit their sorts individually, the instructor reveals the correct categorization and briefly highlights the two or three most commonly misplaced assets in a short written or recorded debrief note. Students are then asked: 'Which category do you think youth ministry tends to over-invest in, and what's the cost?' and post a two-sentence reply. This creates a simple but memorable cognitive hook for the external/internal distinction.
activities.materials: Pre-built card-sort activity in Padlet, Mentimeter, Google Slides (with assigned slides), or native LMS quiz tool; answer key ready for instructor debrief post
activities.learningGoal: Reinforces the distinction between external and internal assets and surfaces assumptions about where faith formation energy is typically directed
activities.scalingNotes: Asynchronous card-sorts work identically regardless of cohort size. If the tool allows, display aggregated class results so students can see where disagreement clustered.

activities.title: Creed, Community, Call, Hope: Case Study Gallery
activities.description: The instructor pre-posts four short anonymous vignettes in the course app — each one a 100-word sketch of a teenager's faith life — and assigns each student one of the four marks of Consequential Faith from Kenda Creasy Dean (creed, community, call, or hope). Students read all four vignettes but analyze them specifically through the lens of their assigned mark: Where is this mark present or absent in each teenager's story? Students post their analysis (3-5 sentences) as a reply to each vignette thread. After all students have posted, they are required to read at least two classmates' analyses on a different mark and reply with one thing that surprised or challenged them. The instructor closes the gallery with a brief synthesis note connecting the four marks back to the internal assets category. This mimics a peer-learning gallery walk adapted for async online learning.
activities.materials: Four pre-written vignettes posted as separate discussion threads in the course app; mark assignments communicated at the start of the activity (e.g., via a numbered list students self-select or are assigned by roster)
activities.learningGoal: Applies Kenda Creasy Dean's four marks of Consequential Faith to concrete adolescent scenarios and builds students' ability to read a young person's faith through this framework
activities.scalingNotes: With 25 students, approximately 6-7 students will share each mark, producing rich multi-perspective threads. For cohorts under 16, assign two marks per student. For larger cohorts, add a fifth vignette.

activities.title: Flip the Frame: Deficit vs. Asset Language Rewrite
activities.description: The instructor posts five real-sounding statements written in 'youth-as-problem' deficit language — the kind often heard in church, school, or community settings (e.g., 'Teenagers today are so apathetic about faith,' 'Kids in this neighborhood have no respect'). Students choose any two statements and rewrite them using asset-framework thinking — shifting from 'what is wrong with youth' to 'what assets are missing or underdeveloped, and who is responsible for providing them.' Students post their rewrites in a shared thread. Peers are then asked to react with a simple emoji or one-word response to each rewrite — 'stronger,' 'still deficit,' 'nuanced' — creating lightweight accountability without requiring lengthy replies. The instructor highlights two or three standout rewrites in a closing comment and names the theological implication: asset-language restores dignity to young people and redistributes responsibility to adults and institutions.
activities.materials: Five pre-written deficit-language statements posted by instructor; discussion thread with emoji-reaction enabled or a simple reply convention established
activities.learningGoal: Trains students to actively reframe dominant cultural narratives about adolescents using the developmental assets paradigm
activities.scalingNotes: Scales easily to any size. For a cohort larger than 40, add a second round where students identify which of the eight asset categories is implicated in each original deficit statement.

activities.title: Asset Gap Map: Designing for a Real Community
activities.description: Students are given a one-paragraph description of a fictional but realistic youth context (e.g., a mid-sized suburban church youth group with strong programming but weak family engagement and almost no ties to the broader neighborhood). Working individually, students review the eight asset categories and identify which two or three categories are most likely underdeveloped in that context — then propose one concrete, actionable step a youth worker could take in the next 30 days to begin building assets in each gap area. Students post their gap maps and proposals to a shared discussion board formatted with clear headers. Once everyone has posted, students are asked to find one classmate whose proposals differ significantly from their own and write two sentences explaining whether the difference reflects a different reading of the context or a different theory of change. The instructor summarizes the range of proposals in a closing note, naming what the diversity of responses reveals about asset-building strategy.
activities.materials: Pre-written community context vignette (150-200 words) posted in course app; discussion board with structured posting template (Gap 1 / Gap 2 / Gap 3 / Proposed Actions)
activities.learningGoal: Moves students from asset identification to asset-building strategy, practicing the kind of contextual analysis they will use in actual ministry settings
activities.scalingNotes: Works well at 25 students with a single shared context. For larger cohorts, use two different community contexts and split the group so each subgroup has richer peer comparison. For smaller cohorts under 15, add a second vignette for individual variety.

activities.title: The 40 Assets Rapid-Fire Debate: Which Matter Most for Faith?
activities.description: The instructor posts a provocative prompt to the course discussion board: 'If a youth ministry could only intentionally cultivate FIVE of the 40 Developmental Assets, which five would produce the most spiritually resilient teenagers — and why?' Each student posts their top five with a two-to-three sentence rationale. Students must then read at least three classmates' lists and reply to one person whose list overlaps with theirs least — identifying the sharpest point of disagreement and making a brief case for their own choices. The instructor closes the activity by naming the assets that appeared most frequently across the class, those that appeared least, and reflecting on what the group's collective choices reveal about their implicit theology of youth ministry and formation. This activity is intentionally designed to generate productive disagreement, not consensus.
activities.materials: Discussion board thread with the debate prompt pre-posted; optional: instructor creates a live results tally using a Mentimeter word cloud or Google Form where students enter their five assets before posting rationale
activities.learningGoal: Challenges students to prioritize within the framework, surfaces differing assumptions about what drives faith development, and connects the asset research to theological convictions about ministry
activities.scalingNotes: The more students participate, the richer the disagreement. For cohorts under 15, ask each student to reply to two classmates instead of one. For larger cohorts, consider creating sub-threads grouped by internal vs. external assets to keep discussion focused.

slides.title: What Does It Take to Grow Up Healthy?
slides.bodyText: If we've been asking 'what's wrong with teenagers?' — we've been asking the wrong question entirely.
slides.speakerNotes: Welcome students to Session 9. Tell them: today we're doing two things at once — learning a research framework AND fundamentally changing how we see young people. Preview the arc: we'll start by challenging our default assumptions about youth, then unpack the 40 Developmental Assets framework, and finally connect it all to what Kenda Creasy Dean calls 'consequential faith.' Let the opening question sit for a beat before moving on.

slides.title: Rethinking Youth
slides.bodyText: Every framework for youth ministry begins with a theory of the problem — so what is the problem, exactly?
slides.speakerNotes: Transition: Before we can talk about solutions — programs, curricula, mission trips, small groups — we need to get our diagnosis right. Most of the time, youth workers and institutions implicitly treat adolescence itself as the problem. Today we challenge that. This section asks: what if we've had the wrong frame all along?

slides.title: The Default Frame in American Culture
slides.bodyText: 'Adolescence has come to be seen as a period of storm and stress — a developmental problem to be contained rather than a human flourishing to be supported.'
slides.speakerNotes: Ask students: where do they see this attitude in popular culture, in schools, in church? Think about how news coverage talks about teens — crime, phones, mental health crises. The dominant cultural narrative frames young people as inherently volatile or risky. Point out that this is not a neutral observation — it shapes policy, church programming, and adult-youth relationships. Ask: if we build youth ministry on this assumption, what does it look like?

slides.title: The Reframe That Changes Everything
slides.bodyText: Young people don't have behavior problems — they have asset deficits. Fix the deficit, and you change the trajectory.
slides.speakerNotes: Pause here. Let this land. This is the conceptual pivot of the entire session. Ask students to react: does this feel true? What does it feel like to be seen as a problem versus someone who is under-resourced? This reframe is not soft or sentimental — it's evidence-based. The Search Institute spent decades documenting exactly which resources (assets) correlate with thriving, and exactly how the absence of those assets correlates with risk. The implications for youth ministry are enormous.

slides.title: The 40 Developmental Assets
slides.bodyText: Search Institute identified 40 concrete building blocks — drawn from decades of research — that young people need to grow up healthy, caring, and responsible.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition into the framework itself. Tell students: Search Institute is a Minneapolis-based research organization that has surveyed millions of young people across the U.S. The 40 Assets are not aspirational guesses — they are empirically identified factors that distinguish thriving adolescents from struggling ones. The framework divides into two major categories — external and internal — each containing four sub-categories. We'll walk through all eight.

slides.title: External Assets: What Communities Provide
bullets: Support — family warmth, caring adults, a neighborhood that notices
Empowerment — youth feel valued, safe, and useful to their community
Boundaries & Expectations — clear rules, high standards, positive peer norms
Constructive Use of Time — structured activities, creative programs, faith communities
slides.speakerNotes: Emphasize: external assets are things done TO and FOR young people by the adults and institutions around them. They require adult action. Ask: which of these does your church or youth program actively provide? Which do families in your community struggle to provide? Note that 'constructive use of time' explicitly includes time in faith communities — this is where the church has a direct, named role in the research. Give a concrete example: a teenager with three caring adults outside the family is measurably more likely to finish school and avoid risky behavior.

slides.title: External vs. Internal Assets
bullets: Provided by families, communities, and institutions
Visible support structures adults can build
Require intentional adult and organizational action
The 'scaffolding' around a young person's development
slides.bodyText: ["Developed within the young person over time", "Values, identity, competencies, and sense of purpose", "Require sustained inner growth and reflection", "The 'foundation' inside a young person's identity"]
slides.speakerNotes: Walk through both columns side by side. Key point: neither category works well without the other. A young person can have tremendous internal assets — integrity, a positive identity — but without external support, those assets are constantly under siege. Conversely, a young person surrounded by supportive adults but lacking internal motivation or values will still struggle. The framework is holistic by design. Ask: which column does your church tend to focus on? Which gets neglected?

slides.title: Internal Assets: What Young People Develop
bullets: Commitment to Learning — motivation, school engagement, homework, reading for pleasure
Positive Values — caring, equality, integrity, responsibility, restraint
Social Competencies — planning, decision-making, cultural competence, conflict resolution
Positive Identity — self-esteem, sense of purpose, optimism about the future
slides.speakerNotes: Note that these internal assets don't appear automatically — they have to be cultivated through relationships, experiences, and time. 'Positive identity' is especially critical in adolescence, which is developmentally the prime season for identity formation (Erikson). Faith communities are uniquely positioned to address all four internal categories: we offer a story (values), a community (social competencies), a calling (sense of purpose), and a hope (optimism about the future). That's not a coincidence — it's a theological argument hiding in plain sight.

slides.title: Search Institute: What the Data Shows
slides.bodyText: Young people with 31–40 assets are 5 to 10 times LESS likely to engage in high-risk behaviors than peers with 10 or fewer assets — and dramatically more likely to report thriving across every measure studied.
slides.speakerNotes: Let the numbers sink in. This isn't a small effect size — it's transformative. The research covered hundreds of thousands of young people across diverse communities. Risk behaviors studied included substance use, violence, early sexual activity, and academic failure. Thriving indicators included school success, volunteer activity, physical health, and — notably — religiosity. Ask: if we could double the average asset count of young people in our community, what would change? What would it cost? What would it take?

slides.title: Consequential Faith
slides.bodyText: Not all faith is created equal — Kenda Creasy Dean asks what kind of faith is actually capable of shaping a whole life.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition: we've seen the research case for assets. Now we zoom in on one asset category that faith communities are uniquely positioned to build — and we get more specific about what kind of faith actually does the work. Kenda Creasy Dean is a professor of youth, church, and culture at Princeton. Her work on 'consequential faith' grows directly out of the National Study of Youth and Religion and draws on the 40 Assets framework. This is where theological and social-scientific thinking converge.

slides.title: The Four Marks of Consequential Faith
bullets: Creed — a defining story teenagers can claim as their own, not just recite
Community — belonging to a people who embody and practice that story together
Call — a sense of vocation: 'my life is for something beyond myself'
Hope — a vision of a future worth living for, grounded in resurrection faith
slides.speakerNotes: Walk through each mark slowly. For CREED: the question is not whether teens can pass a theology quiz, but whether they have a story that shapes their identity. For COMMUNITY: moralistic therapeutic deism (the 'nice God' religion Dean critiques) produces isolated believers, not a community. For CALL: vocation is not career — it's the conviction that your life is on loan for a purpose larger than you. For HOPE: this is not optimism — it's an eschatological orientation rooted in the resurrection. Ask: which of these four does your faith community do well? Which is underdeveloped?

slides.title: What Makes Faith Consequential
slides.bodyText: Faith that doesn't cost anything, change anything, or compel anything — isn't consequential faith. It's a lifestyle accessory.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the phrase students should photograph. Pause after you say it. Dean's concern — rooted in the NSYR data — is that most American teenagers have inherited a watered-down, low-demand version of faith that asks nothing of them and therefore forms nothing in them. Consequential faith is 'thick' — it names the world differently, makes demands, and offers something worth giving your life to. Ask students: what's the difference between a faith that comforts and a faith that compels? Can it be both?

slides.title: Faith Communities as Asset Ecosystems
bullets: Support: intergenerational relationships, family-like belonging
Empowerment: youth as leaders, contributors, and agents of mission
Values & Identity: creed and call form internal assets directly
Hope: the theological asset no secular institution can replicate
slides.speakerNotes: Connect the two frameworks explicitly. Faith communities are not just one asset among forty — they are environments where all eight asset categories can be cultivated at once. The congregation that gets this right becomes a developmental ecosystem, not just a program provider. Ask: is your church currently functioning as an asset ecosystem? Where are the gaps? What would it take to close them? This is practical theology with empirical backing.

slides.title: From Programs to Presence
slides.bodyText: The most powerful thing a faith community can give a young person is not a curriculum — it is adults who know their name and refuse to give up on them.
slides.speakerNotes: This is a gut-check moment for anyone in youth ministry. Search Institute data shows that young people with three or more caring, non-parental adults in their lives are dramatically more likely to thrive. This is a relational asset, not a programmatic one. The implication is uncomfortable: you can run excellent programming and still fail young people if you haven't built real relationships. Dean's consequential faith framework makes the same point — community is a mark of faith precisely because it cannot be replaced by content delivery.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: 40 Assets = a research-based map of what thriving adolescents actually need
External assets come from community; internal assets are built within — both are essential
More assets = less risk and more thriving; the correlation is powerful and consistent
Youth aren't problems to manage — they are people lacking specific, buildable supports
Consequential faith (creed, community, call, hope) addresses the deepest internal assets
slides.speakerNotes: Summarize the session's arc: we started by questioning how we see youth, built up the 40 Assets framework layer by layer, then zoomed in on how consequential faith maps onto — and transcends — the asset model. For reflection or discussion: ask students to identify which of the 40 assets they personally experienced growing up, and which were absent. Then ask: what would it mean to design a youth ministry around closing those gaps rather than filling a calendar? End with a teaser for the next session.

blocks.title: Audiobook
blocks.transcript:
When Danny Agosto was sixteen, local police considered him one of the most dangerous teenagers in Jackson. A gang runner, a dealer, a dropout — at least that is what every label attached to him said. What those labels never captured was the deficit on the other side of the ledger: an absent father, a mother working third shift at a packaging plant forty-five minutes outside the city, a neighborhood without a single safe adult who stayed around long enough to matter. It was not a moral failure that put Danny on those streets. It was a catastrophic shortage of the developmental resources that every young person requires.

Years later, standing at a podium accepting the Jackson Hispanic Chamber of Commerce Legacy Leadership Award, Danny Agosto told the room: "I didn't care about anyone else's life because I didn't value my own. I was just searching for the love and acceptance I never got at home." His mentor Juan Mendoza had not lectured Danny into transformation. He had noticed a gift, offered an opportunity, and supplied what the community had withheld. He had, in the language of the research you are about to engage, built assets. This session will give you a scientific language for what Mendoza did — and a structured framework for doing it intentionally with every young person in your ministry.

[short pause]

Sessions one through eight of DVULI have asked you to look inward — at your calling, your character, your behavioral style, your leadership strategy, the core values you are committing to live by, and the personal patterns that produce breakthrough or breakdown. That interior work was not preliminary to the real work. It was foundational to it. DVULI's conviction is clear: healthy leaders build healthy organizations that serve healthy communities, and no stage of that chain can be skipped.

Session nine marks the pivot outward. Having examined who you are at your best, you are now equipped to look at your youth and your communities through a transformed lens. The first lens DVULI places in your hands is one of the most rigorously validated frameworks in the field of adolescent development: the 40 DEVELOPMENTAL ASSETS, developed by the Search Institute of Minneapolis over five decades of research involving millions of young people across North America and beyond.

[short pause]

Before moving further into the research, take a moment to bring three specific young people from your ministry to mind — one who is clearly thriving, one who participates inconsistently, and one who displays multiple risk behaviors. Hold their faces in your mind as you work through this material. The research will explain far more about the differences between them than you might expect.

[short pause]

Five Decades of Research on What Works.

The Search Institute launched its asset-based research in 1989, initially surveying forty-seven thousand sixth through twelfth graders in one hundred eleven communities across the United States. What they discovered was both simple and paradigm-shifting: the more developmental assets a young person has, the less likely they are to engage in risk behaviors — alcohol and drug use, violence, early sexual activity, school dropout — and the more likely they are to demonstrate what the researchers called THRIVING INDICATORS, including academic achievement, leadership, and helping others. This was the conclusion reached by Benson, Scales, Leffert, and Roehlkepartain in 1999.

This finding, replicated across surveys of more than three million young people in thousands of communities, established what researchers call a dose-response relationship: more assets produce better outcomes, and the relationship is remarkably consistent regardless of gender, geography, race, or socioeconomic background — as Scales and Leffert documented in 2004. Crucially, this is not merely a proprietary Search Institute construct. Scales and Leffert also conducted a comprehensive synthesis of hundreds of peer-reviewed studies and found that each of the forty assets is independently validated by the broader scientific literature on adolescent development. The assets reflect scientific consensus, not branded methodology.

[short pause]

The sobering underside of this finding is equally consistent: most American young people — and particularly young people in under-resourced urban communities — report having far fewer than half of the forty assets. The Search Institute identified thirty-one assets as a threshold associated with robust positive development. In national surveys, the average young person reports having only eighteen to twenty-two, according to Scales and Leffert in 2004. In many of the communities where DVULI leaders serve, that number is lower still. The gap between what young people have and what they need is not a character gap. It is a community resource gap — and that reframe is the central conceptual shift of this session.

[short pause]

The Architecture of the Framework: Eight Categories, Two Domains.

The forty assets are organized into two primary domains — External Assets and Internal Assets — and eight subcategories of four assets each. Understanding this structure is essential for ministry application because it maps directly onto the dual responsibilities of the community and the individual young person.

Let's begin with External Assets — what communities provide.

External assets are the relational and structural building blocks that families, organizations, congregations, schools, and neighborhoods provide to young people. They represent the environmental conditions that either support or undermine healthy development. There are four external asset categories.

The first is Support, which covers assets one through six. This is the experience of being loved, valued, and cared for by family, teachers, other adults, and neighbors. It includes the simple but powerful presence of adults who know a young person's name and communicate positive regard.

The second external category is Empowerment, covering assets seven through ten. This is the experience of being seen as a resource rather than a problem — being asked to contribute, serve, and lead. Safety is also an empowerment asset; young people cannot be empowered in environments where physical threat is constant.

The third category is Boundaries and Expectations, assets eleven through sixteen. This involves consistent, caring, and clearly communicated boundaries at home, school, and in the neighborhood, combined with high expectations from adults who model positive behavior.

The fourth external category is Constructive Use of Time, assets seventeen through twenty. This means three or more hours per week of structured, creative, or enriching activities through religious communities, youth programs, and arts or sports — activities that crowd out the dangerous void of unstructured time.

[short pause]

Now let's turn to Internal Assets — what young people develop.

Internal assets are the values, skills, and self-perceptions that young people build through experience, relationship, and intentional formation. They do not emerge in a vacuum — they are cultivated by the same communities that provide external assets. There are four internal asset categories.

The first is Commitment to Learning, assets twenty-one through twenty-five. This includes motivation to achieve, engagement at school, completion of homework, bonding to educational institutions, and the practice of reading for pleasure.

The second internal category is Positive Values, assets twenty-six through thirty-one. This is the internalization of caring, social justice, integrity, honesty, responsibility, and restraint as lived commitments rather than merely stated ideals.

The third is Social Competencies, assets thirty-two through thirty-six. These are practical skills for navigating relationships and environments — planning, interpersonal communication, cultural competence, the ability to resist negative peer pressure, and peaceful conflict resolution.

The fourth and final category is Positive Identity, assets thirty-seven through forty. This is the bedrock of healthy development: a sense of personal power, healthy self-esteem, a clear sense of purpose, and a positive view of one's own future.

[short pause]

Reading the Results: What Asset Ratings Reveal.

If you were to honestly rate three young people from your ministry across all forty assets — one thriving, one inconsistent, one high-risk — and examine the gap between your thriving youth and your high-risk youth, you would need to ask yourself a fundamental question: is that difference primarily explained by the character of those two young people? Or is it primarily explained by the community resources available to each of them?

Peter Benson, the founder of the Search Institute and architect of the assets framework, argued compellingly in his 2006 book All Kids Are Our Kids that this is not a question about individual moral failure — it is a question about community investment. Thriving youth do not simply decide to thrive. They are embedded in relational and organizational environments that actively build the building blocks of healthy development. Struggling youth are not deficient in character; they are deficient in assets. This is not a diminishment of personal responsibility; it is a clarification of where responsibility lies in the first place.

As Benson wrote: "Asset building is everybody's work. It is the work of families, of congregations, of neighborhoods, of schools, of youth organizations, of employers — of every sector of community life. No single institution can build all forty assets for any young person."

[short pause]

Research by Scales, Roehlkepartain, and Shramko in 2017 adds a particularly important nuance for faith-community leaders: caring adults outside the family — including church leaders, coaches, and mentors — are among the most powerful asset-building forces available to young people, and their impact is disproportionately significant for youth in under-resourced communities where family assets may be limited. Danny Agosto did not need his mentor Juan Mendoza to be everything. He needed Mendoza to be one adult who noticed his gift and stayed around long enough to use it.

As you think about the young people in your own ministry, consider this: which single asset category shows the widest gap between your thriving youth and your highest-risk youth? What specific actions — already within your ministry's capacity — could begin to close that gap within the next thirty days?

[short pause]

The Balance That Cannot Be Ignored: External versus Internal Assets.

One of the most practically important insights of the assets framework is the equal weight given to both external and internal domains. Faith communities sometimes err in one of two directions, and both errors produce predictable failures.

The first error is to focus exclusively on external asset provision — programs, services, safe spaces, food, tutoring — without investing in the development of internal assets. The result, as Benson documented in 2006, is young people who are dependent on external support rather than equipped to navigate life without it. This is the betterment trap that the Community Youth Development framework cautions against: doing things for youth rather than with them.

The second error is to focus exclusively on internal asset development — character formation, faith instruction, values training — while disregarding the environmental conditions that either support or undermine those internal capacities. A teenager who is taught the value of integrity in a Sunday school classroom, then released into a neighborhood with no safety, no adult role models, no structured activities, and no sense that the community values them, is being set up to fail against environmental odds. Internal assets do not fully develop in external deserts.

[short pause]

The research base is unequivocal on this point: both domains matter, and they are mutually reinforcing. Catalano, Berglund, Ryan, Lonczak, and Hawkins, in their 2004 systematic review of twenty-five positive youth development programs, found that the most effective programs were those that addressed both domains simultaneously — integrating relational and organizational provision with the intentional cultivation of values, skills, and identity in young people. The framework calls us to both-and thinking, not either-or programming.

Reflect honestly on your current ministry programming. Does it lean more heavily toward external asset provision or internal asset development? What would need to change to achieve a more intentional balance?

[short pause]

The Central Reframe: From Youth as Problems to Youth as Problem-Solvers Lacking Assets.

Karen Pittman, one of the founding theorists of the Positive Youth Development movement, articulated what has become a foundational axiom of the field: "Problem-free is not fully prepared, and fully prepared is not fully engaged." This statement captures the inadequacy of a deficit-focused approach to youth ministry and youth development. When we define young people primarily by the risk behaviors they display or the problems they present, we are committing a fundamental analytical error: we are treating the symptom as the disease.

The framework developed by Villarruel, Perkins, Borden, and Keith in 2003 for COMMUNITY YOUTH DEVELOPMENT names the alternative: a paradigm in which young people are understood as potential contributors to community health, not as problems to be managed or contained. Community Youth Development is the theoretical and practical synthesis of two streams of evidence — the forty Developmental Assets framework for individual youth development, and the Asset-Based Community Development approach of McKnight and Kretzmann for community-level capacity building. The synthesis holds that every community contributes to the development of its young people, and every young person can offer gifts and experiences in the process of community development and transformation.

[short pause]

This reframe has direct implications for how you talk about your youth, how you design your programs, and how you position young people in relation to your ministry and community. The shift from betterment to development, from doing things for youth to doing things with youth, from seeing young people as the target population to seeing them as the solution strategy — this is not rhetorical repositioning. It is an evidence-based redesign of the entire ministry enterprise.

Benson and Scales, in their 2009 research, extend this reframe further with the concept of adolescent THRIVING — a state qualitatively distinct from merely avoiding risk. Thriving youth, their research found, are characterized by three additional constructs beyond the forty assets: sparks — intrinsic passions and interests that ignite genuine motivation — meaningful relationships with adults who know and nurture those sparks, and genuine empowerment — the actual experience of agency and contribution. Think of Juan Mendoza noticing Danny Agosto's gift for connecting with children at play. That notice was not mere encouragement. It was the identification of a spark, the beginning of a relationship, and an offer of empowerment. It produced transformation.

[short pause]

What Consequential Faith Looks Like in Adolescents.

The forty Developmental Assets framework gives DVULI leaders a research tool. The Consequential Faith framework gives them a theological destination. Drawing on data from the National Study of Youth and Religion, Kenda Creasy Dean in 2010 found that most American teenagers practice what sociologist Christian Smith called Moralistic Therapeutic Deism — a vague religiosity in which God is largely distant, Christianity is primarily about being nice and feeling good, and faith makes no transformative demands on how one lives.

But Dean's research identified a small cohort of teenagers with what she termed CONSEQUENTIAL FAITH — a deeply rooted, genuinely transformative faith that orients every dimension of their lives. What distinguished these young people from their peers was not more knowledge or more devotional discipline. It was the presence of four specific markers.

The first is a Creed to Believe — the ability to articulate beliefs about a God who is both personal and powerful; a theology robust enough to be stated and defended.

The second is a Community to Belong To — relationships with adults who speak with them about issues of life and faith, not just at them; a belonging that is more than attendance.

The third is a Call to Live Out — a life oriented by divine vocation and service to others, not merely the pursuit of self-satisfaction.

The fourth is a Hope to Hold Onto — a sense that their lives are part of a larger story that is going somewhere good, guided by God.

[short pause]

Notice the structural parallel between Dean's four markers and the forty Developmental Assets. Creed maps directly onto Commitment to Learning and Positive Values — the internalization of a framework that interprets experience. Community maps onto the entire External Assets domain — the relational provision that makes belonging possible. Call maps onto Empowerment and Positive Identity — the sense of contribution and purpose that produces agency. And Hope maps onto the most foundational Positive Identity asset: a positive view of personal future. A ministry that intentionally builds the forty assets is simultaneously — and not coincidentally — building the conditions for Consequential Faith.

Dean's 2010 diagnostic of most American youth ministry is sobering: the reason teenagers practice a thin, vague faith rather than consequential faith is not primarily their own theological confusion. It is, Dean argues, because that is the faith the church modeled to them. Thin theology produces thin faith. A community that cannot articulate its own creed cannot form young people in theirs. This is one reason DVULI's first eight sessions attended so carefully to the interior life of the leader — because you cannot give what you do not possess.

[short pause]

Consider the four elements of Consequential Faith — Creed, Community, Call, and Hope. Which of these is your ministry most consistently providing for its young people? Which is most consistently absent?

[short pause]

Implications for Your Ministry: Becoming an Asset Champion.

Benson in 2006 introduced the concept of the ASSET CHAMPION — an individual who makes the intentional building of developmental assets a consistent and visible priority in every interaction with young people. Asset champions are not primarily program directors; they are people who understand that an encouraging word to a teenager in the hallway, a deliberate conversation about future possibilities after a Sunday service, or a job responsibility handed to an adolescent who has never been trusted with one — all of these are asset-building acts. Scale, in this framework, is not primarily about program size. It is about the cumulative relational investment of adults who have decided to see young people differently.

For DVULI participants, the implications cascade in several directions simultaneously. At the individual youth level, the assets framework challenges you to identify specific, targeted relational investments based on honest assessment of each young person's assets. At the organizational level, it challenges you to audit your programs and ask: are asset-building goals explicit in your mission and vision? Do your programs actively promote asset building? Are your youth partners in governance and leadership? At the community level, it challenges you to map the asset-building landscape of your neighborhood — identifying which institutions are already building which assets, where the most critical gaps exist, and how your ministry can collaborate rather than compete in filling them. This is the insight that Villarruel and colleagues articulated in 2003.

[short pause]

This community-level vision is precisely what the Jackson DVULI cohort attempted with their proposal for the Windsor Court Community Center. Reverend Leroy Burnett, Danny Agosto, Randall Jones, and Sandra Cestero were not simply relocating their existing programs into a renovated building. They were trying to create an integrated, neighborhood-wide asset-building ecosystem — one in which young people from the community would move along a developmental pathway from recipients to resources to assets to leaders. That pathway is the forty assets framework made operational.

[short pause]

The Leader's Role: Closing the Loop from Sessions One through Eight.

The DVULI Personal Assessment Model defined three dimensions of the leader's interior formation: Calling — faithfully doing the work God called you to — Character — consistently living out the values you espouse — and Commitment to Change — willingly adjusting to align with God's will. Session nine demonstrates why all three dimensions matter for the young people in your care.

A leader who knows their calling can articulate it with conviction — and that conviction creates the kind of modeling that activates Positive Values and Positive Identity assets in youth. A leader who embodies character provides the adult role model that asset fourteen requires. A leader committed to change can build the learning orientation — the growth mindset — that activates Commitment to Learning assets. The behavioral assessment you completed reveals how you naturally communicate and engage; that knowledge enables you to reach youth whose temperaments differ from yours. The Leadership Strategy Assessment you completed identifies how you develop others; that insight equips you to match your leadership approach to where each young person is on their developmental path.

[short pause]

None of the interior work of sessions one through eight was separate from what you are doing in this session. It was, all along, preparation for this moment: the moment when you turn toward the young people in your community with the knowledge of who you are, the values you will live by, and a research-validated framework for understanding what they need. That is what a DVULI breakthrough looks like from the outside — not a larger budget or a newer building, but a leader who sees young people differently and acts on what they see.

[short pause]

Let's draw together the key takeaways from this session.

First, the forty Developmental Assets represent five decades of validated research establishing a dose-response relationship: more assets produce more thriving and fewer risk behaviors in young people, regardless of race, geography, or gender.

Second, External Assets — what communities provide — and Internal Assets — what young people develop — must be cultivated simultaneously. Overinvestment in either at the expense of the other produces predictable developmental failures.

Third, the critical reframe of Community Youth Development shifts the analysis from individual youth moral failure to community resource deficit — a shift with direct implications for how you design programs, assign leadership roles, and communicate about youth.

Fourth, Kenda Creasy Dean's Consequential Faith framework identifies four markers — Creed, Community, Call, and Hope — that structurally overlap with the forty Developmental Assets, showing that the conditions for faith formation and the conditions for healthy development are, at their core, the same conditions.

Fifth, caring adults outside the family — including faith community leaders and mentors — are among the most powerful asset-building forces available to youth in under-resourced communities, with especially significant impact where family assets are limited.

And sixth, the interior formation work of sessions one through eight was not preliminary to your community ministry; it was foundational to it. Healthy leaders are the proximate cause of healthy asset-building environments for youth.

[short pause]

Looking ahead, the next session will extend the outward pivot begun here by introducing the tools of Community Capacity Building — the Asset-Based Community Development framework of McKnight and Kretzmann, community asset mapping, and the principles of building from the inside out. You will connect the asset assessment work from this session to a neighborhood asset map you will construct with your youth — identifying the organizational, associational, and institutional assets in your community that can collectively produce the developmental ecosystem your young people need.

blocks.title: Infographic
blocks.prompt:
Create a polished 16:9 landscape educational infographic on a light cream background (#faf8f5) titled "THE 40 DEVELOPMENTAL ASSETS FRAMEWORK" in bold dark navy (#00558C) at the top center, with a subtitle beneath in smaller italic gray text reading "What Does It Take to Grow Up Healthy?"

The infographic is divided into two major halves by a subtle vertical dashed line down the center. The left half is labeled "EXTERNAL ASSETS" with a banner in #00558C, subtitled "What Communities Provide" in small muted text. The right half is labeled "INTERNAL ASSETS" with a banner in #319B41, subtitled "What Young People Develop" in small muted text.

On the left side, arrange four rounded rectangular cards stacked vertically, each with a small simple icon and label: (1) a house icon labeled "SUPPORT," (2) a megaphone icon labeled "EMPOWERMENT," (3) a clock icon labeled "BOUNDARIES & EXPECTATIONS," (4) a calendar icon labeled "CONSTRUCTIVE USE OF TIME." Each card has a thin left border in #00558C.

On the right side, arrange four matching rounded rectangular cards: (1) a book icon labeled "COMMITMENT TO LEARNING," (2) a compass icon labeled "POSITIVE VALUES," (3) a people icon labeled "SOCIAL COMPETENCIES," (4) a star icon labeled "POSITIVE IDENTITY." Each card has a thin left border in #319B41.

From both columns, draw converging arrows downward into a central rounded box at the bottom center with a warm amber (#d97706) border, containing the text "ADOLESCENT THRIVING" in bold. Inside this box, list four small pill-shaped badges reading "Creed," "Community," "Call," and "Hope" — representing Kenda Creasy Dean's four marks of Consequential Faith.

In the bottom-left corner, include a small callout box with a contrasting background showing the paradigm shift: a crossed-out phrase "Youth as Problems" in faded red text with an arrow pointing to "Youth as Problem-Solvers Lacking Assets" in bold #319B41 text.

Use clean sans-serif typography throughout, flat design icons, generous white space, soft drop shadows on cards, and a professional academic aesthetic. Color palette strictly uses #00558C as primary, #319B41 as secondary, #d97706 as warm accent, with #faf8f5 background and white (#ffffff) card fills.

config.concept: 40 Developmental Assets and their relationship to adolescent thriving
config.description: A structured rating interface organized into the eight asset categories, with all 40 individual assets listed. Participants rate three youth profiles (selected from their own ministry: one thriving, one inconsistent, one high-risk) on each asset using a 3-point scale (3 = clearly present, 2 = somewhat present, 1 = clearly lacking). As ratings are entered, a side-by-side bar chart for each asset category builds, showing the three youth profiles in different colors. The widget calculates Total External Assets and Total Internal Assets for each youth and generates a gap-analysis summary: 'Youth C has 14 fewer External Asset points than Youth A — here are the three categories with the widest gaps.' The chart is downloadable for use in ministry planning conversations.
config.rationale: Rating actual youth from participants' real ministry contexts — not hypothetical characters — ensures that the 40 Assets framework is encoded through direct application to known human beings, making the abstractions immediately concrete and ministry-relevant rather than academically interesting.

blocks.title: External vs. Internal Assets Balance Visualizer

config.concept: External vs. Internal Assets: community provision versus personal development
config.description: An interactive scale widget (visually rendered as a balance scale) where participants drag asset cards from a shuffled pile of all 40 assets onto either the left pan (External — things the community provides) or the right pan (Internal — things the young person develops). The scale tips based on how many assets the participant places in each pan. After all 40 are sorted, the widget reveals the actual 20/20 split, highlights any assets the participant misclassified, and shows why the balance matters: a community that overinvests in External Assets without developing Internal ones produces dependent young people, while a ministry that focuses only on Internal Assets while neglecting External provision sets young people up for failure against environmental odds.
config.rationale: The balance scale metaphor makes the equilibrium principle visceral and spatial — participants feel the scale tip as they sort cards, encoding through physical interaction the insight that a sustainable asset-development approach requires both community investment and individual growth.

config.concept: Consequential Faith and the four marks of deeply rooted adolescent faith
config.description: A four-quadrant interactive grid labeled with Kenda Creasy Dean's four elements of consequential faith (Creed, Community, Call, Hope). Participants are guided through four ministry program examples from their own context (they type brief descriptions of four programs or activities) and then drag each into the quadrant it primarily develops. A gap analysis reveals which elements of consequential faith their current ministry under-develops. The widget then suggests one concrete programming adjustment for each underrepresented element, drawn from the DVULI framework and illustrated with examples from urban youth ministry contexts like The Jackson Times case study communities.
config.rationale: Applying the four-element framework to participants' actual existing programs surfaces the critical insight that many youth ministries develop Creed extensively but neglect Call and Hope — a pattern that produces theologically informed but community-disengaged young people.
